# RandNLA CACM "What Is Not Proved" Ledger

This ledger tracks every result named in Section 7 of
`docs/RandNLA_CACM_Formalization_Summary.pdf`, and the full-paper audit
requested for the CACM RandNLA survey.  Its purpose is to keep the
formalization honest: a result remains open until the Lean repository contains
a theorem proving it from local foundations, not merely a theorem that assumes
the desired concentration, perturbation, rank, optimization, or solver
statement.

The front-loaded source-chain plan for incomplete paper proofs is maintained in
[`RANDNLA_CACM_PROOF_SOURCE_LEDGER.md`](RANDNLA_CACM_PROOF_SOURCE_LEDGER.md).
That ledger is now part of the full-paper gate: before adding new hard proof
infrastructure for an open row, the relevant primary sources, theorem numbers,
route choice, and local Lean target must be recorded there.

Reference: Petros Drineas and Michael W. Mahoney, "RandNLA: Randomized
Numerical Linear Algebra," Communications of the ACM 59(6), 80--90, 2016.
https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/2842602

## Completion Rule

An item can be moved to `Closed` only when all of the following are true.

- The source-paper theorem statement has been translated into precise Lean
  definitions.
- The probability space, random variables, event, constants, and failure
  probability are explicit.
- The proof does not assume the target theorem, the target concentration
  inequality, or an unnamed good event.
- Any floating-point variant is derived by composing an exact theorem with a
  proved local stability theorem.
- `lake build` and `lake env lean examples/LibraryLookup.lean` pass.
- The theorem PDF, README, library lookup, and executable lookup agree with the
  Lean theorem names.
- Two consecutive weak-component audits find no hidden hypothesis or prose/API
  mismatch for the item.

For autonomous full-paper requests, an open row is also a continuation target:
after recording a failed gate, the next action is to select the highest-priority
open row and prove the next reusable Lean foundation for it.  Do not stop with
only the statement that the row is large or difficult unless the user asked for
a status-only report or a genuine mathematical choice blocks progress.

## Full-Paper Algorithm and Analysis Inventory

This table is the full-paper extraction pass.  It separates the three explicit
meta-algorithms from the application-level algorithms and theorem templates
described later in the article.

Current probability convention for this inventory: all sampling probabilities
and sampling laws are exact mathematical inputs.  The ledgers therefore do not
open obligations for probability construction, normalization, repair,
cumulative/alias sampling, or law-transfer from rounded probability tables.
This is the only exception.  Every non-probability object computed by an
algorithm remains in scope for an FP/inexact-arithmetic theorem, certificate, or
visible not-proved obligation: scale denominators, row/entry scaling,
preconditioners, transforms, computed bases, singular vectors, projection
matrices, sketches, Gram/RHS products, solver inputs, and returned outputs.

| Source item | Exact/randomized analysis in the paper | Current Lean status | Remaining gap |
|---|---|---|---|
| Algorithm 1, element-wise sampling, equation (1) | Independent entry samples with exact probabilities `p_ij`; rescaled estimator is elementwise unbiased. Squared-magnitude probabilities lead to spectral-norm residual concentration, equation (2). | The exact update, floating-point update, trace sketch, hit counter, squared-magnitude exact laws, support-inclusive exact unbiasedness, deterministic FP stability for sampled-entry rescaling/accumulation, Markov/Chebyshev/Chernoff hit-count concentration, residual-as-sum-of-zero-mean-increments, one-step variance-proxy bounds, trace/Loewner variance proxies, source-sharp square vector/transpose-vector variance proxies, source-sharp square dilation Loewner/C-star variance, fixed-vector residual Markov control, finite-cover support under an explicit unit-ball-cover hypothesis, product-grid cover reduction/cardinality from one-dimensional interval grids, Frobenius/Markov residual control, scalar entrywise/union-bound residual control, product-law scalar MGF, finite-family self-adjoint-dilation quadratic-form MGF support, support-aware pointwise MGF adapters for zero-mass samples, truncated finite-test dilation MGF support, one-sided dilation-Loewner event transfer, Hermitian eigenvalue/Loewner/matrix-exponential/CFC trace foundations, positive and negative repository-native matrix-exponential trace diagonalization, upper-/lower-/two-sided trace-exponential/eigenvalue Markov interfaces, sharp square truncated trace-MGF/eigenvalue tail skeletons, generic and source-sharp Bennett-radius exact truncated theorems, source denominator/sample-budget simplification, deterministic truncation transfer, support-aware derived FP gamma-budget transfer, explicit scalar-radius FP corollaries replacing the internal budget matrix by dimension/unit-roundoff terms, and spectral transfer from exact residual events are formalized. | The cited square Drineas--Zouzias source-aligned equation (2) route is closed for exact and FP arithmetic with an explicit FP scalar radius. The only remaining Algorithm 1 variant is the untruncated/general-rectangular CACM-prose statement, if kept in scope separately. |
| Algorithm 2, row sampling, equations (3)--(5) | Independent row samples with exact probabilities `p_i`; norm-squared probabilities make `E[tilde A^T tilde A] = A^T A`; equation (5) gives a Frobenius Gram error bound in expectation and high probability. | The exact sampled sketch, concrete computed denominator routine `fl_sqrt(fl_mul s p_i)`, rounded row scaling, effective relative denominator/division error, Gram estimator, one-entry unbiasedness, squared-Frobenius second moment, high-probability Markov equation (5), computed-denominator scaling-only FP equation (5), and computed-denominator fully computed Gram-dot-product FP equation (5) are formalized. | Spectral-norm improvements from Khintchine or matrix Bernstein are not formalized. Alternative denominator routines require their own `ComputedRowScaleDen` constructors; probability construction remains intentionally exact by convention. |

denominator, and computed left/right/two-sided preconditioner error bounds reuse local matrix-multiplication, finite-probability, leverage-score, and FP dot-product theorems. | The SRHT/signed-Hadamard branch is closed at the exact-law and selected actual-input concrete-denominator FP endpoint, and the selected finite signed-mixing plus CountSketch downstream row-sampling endpoints now instantiate the same square-root-times-reciprocal-square-root denominator routine at output dimension `r`. Still open: concrete fast SRHT/FHT storage/apply certificates beyond the closed functional/generated/writeback paths, storage certificates beyond the three modeled rounded-copy paths for signs or basis tables, concrete QR/SVD/singular-vector routines instantiating `ComputedMatrix`, denominator routines beyond the five closed exact-input, division-then-sqrt, reciprocal-multiply-then-sqrt, split-square-root, and square-root-times-reciprocal-square-root certificates, and stronger non-SRHT Gaussian/FJLT/input-sparsity random-transform uniformization such as optimal CountSketch subspace-embedding concentration. |
| Least-squares sketching, equation (8) | Sampling/projection sketches return `1 ± epsilon` relative-error LS solutions; sketches can also build preconditioners for high-precision iterative solvers. | The deterministic implication from a residual-objective-preserving sketch to an LS objective bound is formalized. Coordinate-space bridges now handle both the older operator-2 Gram-error event and the sharper two-sided finite-Loewner event. The concrete sampled-row LS theorem uses canonical residual coordinates `U^T(Ax-b)`. Column/RHS coordinate adapters reduce the residual condition to representing `[A b]`, and the augmented-span theorem constructs an orthonormal basis of `span{columns(A), b}`, proves coordinates for `[A b]`, and closes the concrete sampled-row theorem at dimension `finrank span{columns(A), b}`. The source-aligned exact Bennett sample-budget theorem for equation (8) is now formalized as `leverageTraceProbability_eventProb_rowSampleLSObjective_le_one_add_eta_of_augmentedSpan_sample_budget`; the rounded-Gram FP transfer is `leverageTraceProbability_eventProb_fl_lsObjective_le_one_add_eta_of_augmentedSpan_sample_budget`. The literal rounded sampled-row `A,b` construction is modeled by `fl_rowSampleLSMatrixWithBasisScale` and `fl_rowSampleLSVectorWithBasisScale`, with rowwise residual perturbation, deterministic objective perturbation, high-probability rounded-minimizer composition under an explicit objective-budget condition, an additive solver-gap high-probability transfer, a concrete normal-equations/Cholesky solver transfer, and visible-domain stored-QR/preconditioner handoffs under explicit local diagonal-dominance/row-budget, nonbreakdown, compact-smallness, active-pivot/scalar-comparison, and triangular-solve hypotheses. The identity-basis fallback is available in exact and FP-transfer forms with uniform equation (6) probabilities and `d = m`. | The generic claim that an arbitrary computed QR/preconditioner loop supplies the visible source/domain fields is not proved. Random-projection versions of equation (8) remain separate. |
| Low-rank approximation, equation (9) | A structural condition involving right singular subspaces and a sketching matrix implies good low-rank approximation. | No full Lean theorem yet. | Requires rectangular SVD, rank, projections, pseudoinverses, best rank-`k` approximation, and unitarily invariant norms. |
| Matrix completion, equations (10)--(11) | Uniform or leverage-like entry sampling plus nuclear-norm minimization recovers or approximates a low-rank matrix with high probability under incoherence-style assumptions. | No full Lean theorem yet. | Requires rank/incoherence, nuclear norm, convex optimization/minimization, sampling model, and recovery certificate machinery. |
| Laplacian-based linear solvers | Edge/effective-resistance sampling sparsifies a graph Laplacian and supports nearly-linear-time preconditioned solves. | Existing generic iterative-refinement and stationary-iteration material is unrelated to effective-resistance sparsification. | Requires graph Laplacians, incidence matrices, effective resistance/leverage equivalence, spectral sparsification, and solver complexity/stability interfaces. |
| Kernel/statistics/machine-learning applications | RandNLA sketches support kernel approximation, classification, clustering, and related downstream tasks. | No theorem target has been extracted beyond the narrative survey claims. | Needs a narrowed mathematical target before formalization. |

These results close deterministic pieces of the Section 7 backlog.  They do not
close the full paper-level rows below unless the corresponding randomized or
structural theorem has also been proved.

## Historical Bottleneck Ledger

There is currently no active red bottleneck for the cited square
source-aligned Algorithm 1 theorem.  The long `A1.5-B1` table below is
historical: later rows 360--361 close the source sample-budget, deterministic
truncation, support-aware FP gamma-budget transfer, and the explicit scalar
FP-radius corollaries.  Progress on future
full-paper runs should select a new open row from the table above unless the
user explicitly asks for the optional untruncated/general-rectangular Algorithm
1 variant.

- If a result is partly proved, add a new row for the proved subtheorem and
  leave the original paper theorem open until the full statement is formalized.
- Do not delete an open row simply because it is difficult.  Move it only when
  its Lean theorem names and validation commands are recorded.
- Treat each open row as an ordered proof backlog item.  A failed full-paper
  gate should immediately produce the next frontier theorem target, expected
  file/module, reused local foundations, and validation command, then proof work
  should continue unless the user explicitly pauses the run.
